shortDescription.text: Swig autoescape explicitly disabled, enabling stored/reflected XSS
Deprecated Swig engine with autoescape off may allow server-side template injection
Use of deprecated and unmaintained Swig template engine
Security hardening mechanisms are commented out

message.text: Swig template engine autoescape is explicitly set to `false` at line 119. All template variables rendered via `{{ variable }}` will output raw HTML without escaping. Any user-controlled data passed to templates (e.g., usernames, profile fields, form inputs) will be rendered as-is, enabling both stored and reflected Cross-Site Scripting. This is the single most impactful finding: every view in `app/views/` inherits this unsafe default. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript through any input field that is subsequently rendered in a template.

With autoescape disabled and Swig as the template engine, server-side template injection (SSTI) becomes a risk if any user input is concatenated into template strings before rendering (rather than passed as context variables). Swig supports arbitrary expression evaluation in `{{ }}` tags. If route handlers construct template strings dynamically with user input, an attacker could inject Swig expressions to execute arbitrary code on the server. Even without direct string concatenation, the combination of disabled autoescape and a deprecated/unmaintained engine (no security patches) significantly elevates SSTI risk.

Swig (^1.4.2) has been deprecated since 2016 and is unmaintained. The library has known security issues that will never be patched. The npm page explicitly warns against using it in production. This constitutes OWASP A06:2021 - Vulnerable and Outdated Components. The application should migrate to an actively maintained template engine such as Nunjucks (API-compatible successor), EJS, or Handlebars.

Session cookies lack `httpOnly` and `secure` flags (lines 79-99). Without `httpOnly`, XSS payloads executed via the disabled autoescape can trivially steal session tokens via `document.cookie`. Without `secure`, cookies are transmitted over plaintext HTTP. This compounds the XSS finding: exploiting the template XSS gives immediate session hijacking capability.

CSRF protection via `csurf` middleware is imported but entirely commented out (lines 103-109). Without CSRF tokens, attackers can forge cross-origin requests on behalf of authenticated users. Combined with the XSS from disabled autoescape, this creates a complete attack chain: XSS can be used to bypass any client-side CSRF mitigations even if they were present.

All security headers via Helmet are commented out (line 12): no X-Frame-Options (clickjacking), no Content-Security-Policy (XSS mitigation layer), no HSTS (downgrade attacks), no X-Content-Type-Options (MIME sniffing), and x-powered-by header is not disabled (information leakage). These headers form defense-in-depth layers that would partially mitigate the impact of the XSS from disabled autoescape.
